nativeRange.habitat: Seaward reefs over bare rock or mixed rubble and sand; adults typically encountered at depths of 9–46 m, with juveniles tending to shelter in protected bays and lagoons

diet.notes: Wild diet has been documented as the surface film of detritus, diatoms, and fine filamentous algae grazed from sand and bare rock. In captivity, dried marine algae (nori) offered on a clip several times per week is generally advised as a staple. Spirulina, algae-based pellets, and occasional meaty foods such as Mysis or krill have been reported to be accepted. Consistent algae feeding tends to help maintain color, immune function, and reduce nipping tendencies toward corals.

summary: An Indo-Pacific surgeonfish recognized by a vivid orange shoulder bar against a two-tone gray body in adults. Generally considered reef-safe when well fed and is commonly regarded as one of the more peaceful Acanthurus species toward non-conspecifics.
careNotes: Acanthurus olivaceus undergoes a dramatic color change with age: juveniles are uniformly bright yellow with a hint of blue fringing on the fins, while adults develop a pale gray anterior, dark gray-blue posterior, and a distinctive orange shoulder bar outlined in dark gray. Because the juvenile and adult phases look like different fish, juveniles are sometimes mistakenly sold as a different species. Reported adult sizes vary across sources: ReefApp lists about 25 cm, while FishBase records a maximum of 35 cm TL and LiveAquaria notes up to 14 inches (~35.6 cm); the range here spans typical to maximum. Tank-size recommendations also vary widely — LiveAquaria suggests 180 gal (~680 L), ReefApp suggests 800 L, and some hobby sources state 100–135 gal as an absolute minimum; given this fish's active swimming behavior and adult size, a tank in the 700 L+ range is generally advised, with larger preferred for long-term keeping. Tangs are commonly regarded as susceptible to Head and Lateral Line Erosion (HLLE) when kept with poor water quality or insufficient algae-rich diet; vitamin-enriched nori, varied algae, and stable parameters tend to mitigate this risk. As with all surgeonfish, paired caudal-peduncle scalpels are permanently exposed and can injure handlers, netting, and tankmates. Sources disagree on conspecific aggression: LiveAquaria characterizes the species as relatively unaggressive toward other tangs (with juveniles introduced to established adults working best), while specialist sources (ReefApp, hobby community guides) warn against keeping it with other Acanthurus due to hierarchy stress; the cautious approach has been adopted here. Reef-safe rating is generally 'yes' when the fish is well fed, but hungry individuals have been reported to nip at large-polyp stony corals. All trade specimens are wild-caught; captive breeding has not been documented.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported. Wild populations are presumed broadcast spawners typical of Acanthuridae; adult males (≥17 cm) develop a more convex snout profile, which has been used to distinguish sexes in mature individuals.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page for Orangeshoulder Tang (Acanthurus olivaceus). Provided care level, temperament, diet type, reef compatibility, water conditions (sg, temperature, dKH, pH), maximum adult size, family, and minimum tank size recommendation (180 gal). Resolved from the tangs/surgeons collection root via search; original prompt URL pointed to the category page rather than the species page.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific authority (Bloch & Schneider, 1801), taxonomy (Acanthuridae, Acanthuriformes), maximum adult size (35.0 cm TL), maximum reported age (33 years), distribution (Christmas Island and Cocos-Keeling east to Hawaii and Tuamotu, north to Japan, south to Lord Howe), habitat (seaward reefs, bare rock or mixed rubble and sand; juveniles in protected bays and lagoons), depth range (3–46 m, typical 9–46 m), preferred temperature (24–28°C, mean 27.3°C), diet (surface film of detritus, diatoms, fine filamentous algae), and trophic level (2.3).

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked typical adult size (25 cm), minimum tank size (800 L — higher than the LiveAquaria recommendation of ~680 L; final value of 700 L averages between the two with a bias toward the larger figure per playbook tank-sizing rule), reef safety ('always reef safe'), conspecific aggression toward other Acanthurus species, and diet (macroalgae, microalgae, occasional crustaceans). Used as third source to break the LiveAquaria/specialist disagreement on conspecific aggression.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature range (24–28°C, slightly broader than LiveAquaria's 72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C; the final 23–27°C reflects an average), pH (8.1–8.4), and reef safety caveat (will pick at LPS corals if underfed).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reported. Trade specimens are wild-caught. Adult males develop a more pronounced convex snout profile, which has been used to distinguish sexes once specimens exceed ~17 cm.
